summary: Gruber debunks the widely-circulated claim from Samsung's trial defense that Apple 'stole' the iPhone design from Sony. Samsung's pre-trial brief cited an internal Apple mockup where designer Shin Nishibori created a 'Sony-like' phone concept after executives circulated an article about Sony's design philosophy. Gruber points out the critical distinction: this was an Apple designer's interpretation of a broad Sony design description, not a copy of any actual Sony phone. He shows that the real Sony phone Samsung claims Apple copied looks nothing like the iPhone. The post also notes that Apple had its own pre-existing iPhone design concepts from 2005, further undermining the 'stolen from Sony' narrative.
key_points: Samsung's legal argument rested on an internal Apple mockup labeled 'Sony' that was created by Apple designer Shin Nishibori, not on any actual Sony product
The mockup was inspired by a broad description of Sony's design philosophy from a magazine article, not copied from a specific Sony device
The actual Sony phone Samsung claimed Apple copied bears no resemblance to the iPhone
Apple had independent iPhone design concepts dating to 2005, before the Sony-inspired mockup was created
Commentators like Eric Raymond and tech blogs were conflating an internal design exercise with proof of copying
money_quotes: It's not a Sony phone. It's an in-house mockup by an Apple designer inspired by a very broad description of Sony devices.
There is no actual circa 2006 Sony phone that looks like this.
Yeah, that's a dead ringer for the iPhone.
Don't make the mistake of thinking this 'Sony' mockup was an actual Sony phone or even a Sony concept. It was an Apple concept showing what a Sony phone might look like.
key_insight: The 'Apple stole from Sony' narrative collapses when you realize the mockup was an Apple designer's speculative interpretation of Sony's design philosophy, not a copy of any actual Sony product.
